moveAnnotations.annotation: Welcome to the Zukertort Opening, a quiet but ambitious system where we skip the central e4 or d4 pushes that dominate most games. By playing b4, we immediately stake a claim on the queenside and prepare to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2, where it will exert long-term pressure along the long diagonal toward e5 and f6. This move is deceptively flexible—we're not committing to a specific pawn structure yet, which gives us options against Black's responses.

Black stakes an immediate claim in the center with d5, a natural and principled response that controls e4 and prevents us from establishing a strong pawn center. This is the critical test of whether we can maintain pressure with our setup, and it sets up the tension we'll need to resolve with our next move.

Now we develop our knight to its natural square, supporting the center and preparing to challenge Black's d5 pawn while keeping all our options open. Nf3 controls the key squares e5 and d4, and it complements our plan to fianchetto the light-squared bishop—together, these pieces will create pressure along the long diagonal and give us flexibility in how we want to play the middlegame. This move exemplifies the Zukertort philosophy: solid development and long-term piece coordination rather than immediate tactical fireworks.
